keyPointsSummary: 'Wanted' is the simple past tense of 'want' — it is used for desires or wishes in the past.
Formula 1: Subject + wanted + to + base verb (e.g., I wanted to go home.)
Formula 2: Subject + wanted + noun (e.g., She wanted a new book.)
'Wanted' is the same for ALL subjects — I, he, she, we, they, you + wanted (no change).
Negative form: Subject + did not (didn't) + want + to + verb / noun. NOT 'did not wanted'.
Question form: Did + subject + want + to + verb / noun? NOT 'Did + subject + wanted'.
Common mistake: Forgetting 'to' before the verb — 'I wanted go' is WRONG. 'I wanted to go' is CORRECT.
Contrast with Day 11: 'want/wants' = present; 'wanted' = past. Example: 'I want tea' vs 'I wanted tea'.
'Just wanted to say/tell' is a polite phrase used in formal and informal communication.
Indirect speech: 'wants' changes to 'wanted' when reporting in past — 'She said she wanted to meet you.'
Unfulfilled desire: 'I wanted to... but...' is a natural pattern for past desires that could not be fulfilled.
'Wanted someone to do something' = advanced pattern — e.g., 'We wanted him to join the team.'

quickQuiz.answer: He did not want a promotion.
She wanted to become a doctor.

quickQuiz.hindi: क्या वह वहाँ जाना चाहता था?
quickQuiz.answer: Did he want to go there?

quickQuiz.hindi: मैं बस शुक्रिया कहना चाहता था।
quickQuiz.answer: I just wanted to say thank you.

quickQuiz.hindi: उसने कहा कि वह मुझसे मिलना चाहती थी।
quickQuiz.answer: She said she wanted to meet me.

quickQuiz.hindi: मैं तुम्हें call करना चाहता था पर busy था।
quickQuiz.answer: I wanted to call you but I was busy.

quickQuiz.hindi: उन्हें किसी की मदद नहीं चाहिए थी।
quickQuiz.answer: They did not want any help.

quickQuiz.hindi: मैं हमेशा से इस field में काम करना चाहता था।
quickQuiz.answer: I always wanted to work in this field.

quickQuiz.hindi: बचपन में मैं pilot बनना चाहता था।
quickQuiz.answer: As a child, I wanted to become a pilot.

mustRememberRules: Rule 1: 'Wanted to' NOT 'wanted' alone — always add 'to' before a verb: I wanted TO go (✅), I wanted go (❌).
Rule 2: In questions — 'Did + subject + WANT (not wanted)': Did she WANT? (✅), Did she WANTED? (❌).
Rule 3: In negatives — 'did not WANT (not wanted)': I didn't WANT (✅), I didn't WANTED (❌).
Rule 4: 'Wanted' is SAME for all subjects — no 's', no change: I/he/she/we/they all use 'wanted'.
Rule 5: 'Wanted + noun' (no to) vs 'wanted + to + verb' — know which to use based on what follows.
Rule 6: Past context signal — words like yesterday, last year, as a child, at that time tell you to use 'wanted'.
Rule 7: Indirect speech — 'She says she wants' → 'She said she wanted' (backshifting in reported speech).
